results.possibleIdentity: Roger Loft was an artist and master printer active in New York during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known as the founder and director of Chiron Press (active approximately 1968–1971), a studio that produced limited-edition silkscreen prints for prominent contemporary artists. Loft received his B.F.A. from the Tyler School of Art in 1968 and was a key figure in the community associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that paired artists with engineers. Through Chiron Press, Loft collaborated with E.A.T.-affiliated artists such as Robert Rauschenberg and Robert Motherwell. He exhibited his own work at venues including the Whitney Museum (1972) and the Kornblee Gallery (1970). His personal life also connects him to the name 'Merrill' through his wife, Jane Wellington Merrill.

Merrill Greene was a central organizational and administrative figure within Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Serving as the executive coordinator and assistant to E.A.T. co-founder Billy Klüver, Greene was instrumental in managing the organization's multifaceted projects that blended art, science, and engineering. Her work was vital to major E.A.T. initiatives, most notably the design and construction of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. While primarily a coordinator rather than a creative artist, her name appears extensively in the historical records and bibliographies of E.A.T. alongside the artists and engineers she supported, leading to historical citations where her identity is linked to the creative output of the group.
